main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Soft-hard functions (SHFs) to model soft and hard bounds for each objective in multi-objective optimization","Novel minimax optimization formulation to maximize SHF-defined utility under unknown preferences","Two-step process: (1) Multi-objective Bayesian optimization to densely sample the Pareto frontier, (2) Robust submodular function optimization to sparsify the set of Pareto-optimal points"],"problems":["Efficiently selecting a compact, representative set of Pareto-optimal solutions in multi-objective optimization that aligns with decision-maker's (DM's) soft and hard preferences","Reducing the cognitive and computational burden of exploring the full Pareto frontier in expensive, real-world multi-objective optimization settings","Incorporating intuitive, context-specific soft and hard bounds into the multi-objective optimization process"],"datasets":["Real clinical data for cervical cancer brachytherapy treatment planning","Synthetic datasets for multi-objective optimization","Engineering design datasets","Large language model (LLM) personalization datasets","Deep learning model selection datasets"],"metrics":["SHF-defined utility (utility defined by soft-hard functions)","Maximum possible desired utility (relative utility achieved)"],"results":[{"metric":"SHF-defined utility (brachytherapy)","value":">3% greater than next best approach"},{"metric":"Maximum possible desired utility (across experiments)","value":">99% achieved within validation of 5 points"},{"metric":"Maximum possible desired utility (other experiments)","value":"59% achieved within validation of 5 points"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of soft-hard functions (SHFs) to model both soft and hard bounds for each objective in multi-objective optimization, capturing practitioner intuition not previously modeled.","Formulation of a novel minimax optimization problem to maximize SHF-defined utility under unknown preferences.","Development of a two-step process combining multi-objective Bayesian optimization and robust submodular function optimization to obtain a compact, diverse, and near-optimal set of Pareto-optimal points with theoretical guarantees.","Empirical validation on real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating superior performance in utility and compactness of solution sets compared to prior methods."]}

methods: Soft-hard functions (SHFs) to model soft and hard bounds for each objective in multi-objective optimization
Novel minimax optimization formulation to maximize SHF-defined utility under unknown preferences
Two-step process: (1) Multi-objective Bayesian optimization to densely sample the Pareto frontier, (2) Robust submodular function optimization to sparsify the set of Pareto-optimal points
problems: Efficiently selecting a compact, representative set of Pareto-optimal solutions in multi-objective optimization that aligns with decision-maker's (DM's) soft and hard preferences
Reducing the cognitive and computational burden of exploring the full Pareto frontier in expensive, real-world multi-objective optimization settings
Incorporating intuitive, context-specific soft and hard bounds into the multi-objective optimization process
datasets: Real clinical data for cervical cancer brachytherapy treatment planning
metrics: SHF-defined utility (utility defined by soft-hard functions)
Maximum possible desired utility (relative utility achieved)

results.value: >3% greater than next best approach
>99% achieved within validation of 5 points
59% achieved within validation of 5 points

novelty_claims: Introduction of soft-hard functions (SHFs) to model both soft and hard bounds for each objective in multi-objective optimization, capturing practitioner intuition not previously modeled.
Formulation of a novel minimax optimization problem to maximize SHF-defined utility under unknown preferences.

Development of a two-step process combining multi-objective Bayesian optimization and robust submodular function optimization to obtain a compact, diverse, and near-optimal set of Pareto-optimal points with theoretical guarantees.
Empirical validation on real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating superior performance in utility and compactness of solution sets compared to prior methods.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["GPU-based multi-criteria optimization (gMCO) algorithm for HDR prostate brachytherapy","Implementation of two GPU-based optimization engines: simulated annealing (gSA) and quasi-Newton optimizer (gL-BFGS)","Parallel computation of multiple plans using GPU","Selection of preferred optimization engine based on equivalence and computational performance"],"problems":["Manual fine-tuning and lack of patient-specificity in current HDR brachytherapy planning","Time-consuming and planner-dependent planning procedures","Need for faster and more robust inverse planning algorithms for HDR prostate brachytherapy"],"datasets":["562 previously treated prostate HDR cases (100 for validation, 462 for testing)"],"metrics":["Number of clinically valid plans","Target coverage greater than 95%","Mean planning time","Computational speedup (gL-BFGS vs gSA)"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of clinically valid plans (clinical plans)","value":"428/462 (92.6%)"},{"metric":"Number of clinically valid plans (gMCO plans)","value":"461/462 (99.8%)"},{"metric":"Number of valid plans with target coverage >95% (clinical plans)","value":"288/462 (62.3%)"},{"metric":"Number of valid plans with target coverage >95% (gMCO plans)","value":"414/462 (89.6%)"},{"metric":"Mean planning time (gMCO, 1000 plans)","value":"9.4 seconds"},{"metric":"Computational speedup (gL-BFGS vs gSA)","value":"up to 67 times faster"}],"novelty_claims":["First implementation of a GPU-based multi-criteria optimization (gMCO) algorithm for HDR prostate brachytherapy","Ultra-fast computation of thousands of Pareto optimal plans using GPU and gL-BFGS optimizer","Significant improvement in plan quality and reduction in planning time compared to clinical plans","Robust, patient-specific, and fully automated inverse planning without user intervention"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multi-objective decoding (MOD): a decoding-time algorithm that outputs the next token from a linear combination of predictions of all base models, for any given weightings over different objectives","Closed-form solution for MOD derived via Legendre transform from f-divergence regularized alignment approaches (e.g., PPO, DPO)","Efficient decoding strategy for MOD that allows arbitrary objective weightings at inference time","Theoretical analysis of MOD's optimality and error propagation"],"problems":["Aligning language models to multiple human preferences/objectives simultaneously","Existing methods only optimize for a single reward function, limiting adaptability to varied objectives","Difficulty in adapting LMs on-the-fly to different combinations of objectives without retraining","Lack of precise and interpretable control over output characteristics in current methods"],"datasets":["Reddit Summary","Helpful Assistant","Safety Alignment","Toxigen","Codex@1","GSM-COT","BBH-COT"],"metrics":["Overall reward improvement","Toxicity (on Toxigen)","Codex@1","GSM-COT","BBH-COT"],"results":[{"metric":"Overall reward improvement (Helpful Assistant, 3 objectives, MOD vs parameter merging)","value":"12.8% relative improvement"},{"metric":"Toxicity (Toxigen, best MOD configuration)","value":"Reduced to nearly 0%"},{"metric":"Relative improvement across Codex@1, GSM-COT, BBH-COT (best MOD configuration)","value":"7.9% to 33.3%"}],"novelty_claims":["First to achieve decoding-time alignment towards multiple objectives simultaneously via linear combination of base models' predictions","MOD is a training-free, closed-form, and efficient algorithm for multi-objective alignment, generalizing and explaining many existing tuning and decoding strategies","MOD allows arbitrary preference weightings at inference time, avoiding retraining and enabling precise, interpretable control over AI outputs","Theoretical analysis reveals sub-optimality of parameter-merging and provides optimality guarantees for MOD under f-divergence regularization","MOD is applicable to both PPO/DPO and SFT models, and works even with a mix of positive and negative weights"]}

methods: Multi-objective decoding (MOD): a decoding-time algorithm that outputs the next token from a linear combination of predictions of all base models, for any given weightings over different objectives
Closed-form solution for MOD derived via Legendre transform from f-divergence regularized alignment approaches (e.g., PPO, DPO)
Efficient decoding strategy for MOD that allows arbitrary objective weightings at inference time
Theoretical analysis of MOD's optimality and error propagation
problems: Aligning language models to multiple human preferences/objectives simultaneously
Existing methods only optimize for a single reward function, limiting adaptability to varied objectives
Difficulty in adapting LMs on-the-fly to different combinations of objectives without retraining
Lack of precise and interpretable control over output characteristics in current methods

results.metric: Overall reward improvement (Helpful Assistant, 3 objectives, MOD vs parameter merging)
results.value: 12.8% relative improvement
Reduced to nearly 0%

results.metric: Relative improvement across Codex@1, GSM-COT, BBH-COT (best MOD configuration)

novelty_claims: First to achieve decoding-time alignment towards multiple objectives simultaneously via linear combination of base models' predictions
MOD is a training-free, closed-form, and efficient algorithm for multi-objective alignment, generalizing and explaining many existing tuning and decoding strategies
MOD allows arbitrary preference weightings at inference time, avoiding retraining and enabling precise, interpretable control over AI outputs
Theoretical analysis reveals sub-optimality of parameter-merging and provides optimality guarantees for MOD under f-divergence regularization
MOD is applicable to both PPO/DPO and SFT models, and works even with a mix of positive and negative weights

novelty_claims: Proposes a secure health fog (SHF) framework with adaptive model tuning for personalized health recommendations.
Introduces the ZigbeeSHF protocol that combines symmetric and public-key cryptography to secure Zigbee-based IoT health data against a wide range of attacks.
Uses concatenation of encrypted data and digital signatures to ensure data authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality during migration.
Employs formal verification tools (Scyther and AVISPA) to rigorously validate the security of the proposed protocol.
Demonstrates significant accuracy improvement in personalized recommendations through adaptive model tuning.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): a new algorithm for fine-tuning language models from human preferences using a simple binary cross-entropy objective, without explicit reward modeling or reinforcement learning","Comparison with PPO-based RLHF (Proximal Policy Optimization-based Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)"],"problems":["Difficulty in precisely controlling the behavior of large unsupervised language models due to unsupervised training","Complexity and instability of existing RLHF pipelines for aligning language models with human preferences","High computational cost and implementation complexity of RLHF methods"],"datasets":["Human preference datasets for sentiment modulation, summarization, and single-turn dialogue (exact dataset names not specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Sentiment control accuracy","Response quality (summarization and dialogue)","Stability of training","Computational efficiency"],"results":[{"metric":"Sentiment control accuracy","value":"DPO exceeds PPO-based RLHF"},{"metric":"Response quality (summarization and dialogue)","value":"DPO matches or improves over PPO-based RLHF"},{"metric":"Computational efficiency","value":"DPO is substantially simpler and more lightweight than PPO-based RLHF"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a simple, stable, and computationally lightweight algorithm for aligning language models with human preferences without explicit reward modeling or reinforcement learning","DPO enables closed-form extraction of the optimal policy using a simple classification loss, eliminating the need for sampling from the language model during fine-tuning and significant hyperparameter tuning","DPO matches or outperforms existing RLHF methods in key tasks while being much simpler to implement and train"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Flexible framework for multi-objective Bayesian optimization using random scalarizations","Multi-objective extensions of Bayesian optimization algorithms: upper confidence bound (UCB) and Thompson sampling (TS) with random scalarizations","Bayesian approach with user-specified priors over scalarization functions"],"problems":["Efficient multi-objective optimization when function evaluations are expensive","Sampling Pareto optimal points from specific, user-specified regions of the Pareto front rather than the entire front","Incorporating user preferences flexibly into multi-objective Bayesian optimization","Reducing computational cost of multi-objective Bayesian optimization"],"datasets":["Synthetic datasets for multi-objective optimization","Real-life tasks (e.g., drug discovery, neural network design)"],"metrics":["Regret (Bayes regret in the multi-objective setting)","Computational cost (complexity)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret","value":"Sublinear regret achieved by the proposed method"},{"metric":"Computational cost","value":"Scales linearly with the number of objectives K; computationally cheaper than entropy-based methods such as PESMO"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a flexible framework for multi-objective Bayesian optimization using random scalarizations, allowing sampling from arbitrary (including non-convex and non-connected) regions of the Pareto front specified by user preferences.","Extends the class of scalarizations beyond linear to any monotonic and Lipschitz functions, unlike prior work.","Provides theoretical guarantees by proving sublinear regret bounds for the proposed method, which is not available for most existing methods.","Demonstrates computational simplicity: the method scales linearly with the number of objectives, in contrast to exponential scaling in some prior methods.","The approach is fully amenable to interactive settings, allowing dynamic changes in user preferences."]}

methods: Flexible framework for multi-objective Bayesian optimization using random scalarizations
Multi-objective extensions of Bayesian optimization algorithms: upper confidence bound (UCB) and Thompson sampling (TS) with random scalarizations
Bayesian approach with user-specified priors over scalarization functions
problems: Efficient multi-objective optimization when function evaluations are expensive
Sampling Pareto optimal points from specific, user-specified regions of the Pareto front rather than the entire front

datasets: Real-life tasks (e.g., drug discovery, neural network design)
metrics: Regret (Bayes regret in the multi-objective setting)

results.value: Sublinear regret achieved by the proposed method

novelty_claims: Proposes a flexible framework for multi-objective Bayesian optimization using random scalarizations, allowing sampling from arbitrary (including non-convex and non-connected) regions of the Pareto front specified by user preferences.
Extends the class of scalarizations beyond linear to any monotonic and Lipschitz functions, unlike prior work.
Provides theoretical guarantees by proving sublinear regret bounds for the proposed method, which is not available for most existing methods.
Demonstrates computational simplicity: the method scales linearly with the number of objectives, in contrast to exponential scaling in some prior methods.
The approach is fully amenable to interactive settings, allowing dynamic changes in user preferences.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Pareto-frontier entropy search (PFES): a novel entropy-based multi-objective Bayesian optimization method that considers the entropy of the Pareto-frontier and directly considers the entropy of the Pareto-frontier, capturing the essential trade-off relations among objectives in multi-objective optimization","Derivation of an analytical formula for methods: Pareto-frontier entropy search (PFES): a novel entropy-based multi-objective Bayesian optimization method that considers the entropy of the Pareto-frontier and derives an analytical formula for entropy computation using recursive decomposition techniques from Pareto hyper-volume computation
Extension of PFES to the decoupled setting, allowing for separate observation of objective functions by considering the entropy of the marginal density for each output dimension

metrics: Performance of Bayesian optimization (e.g., hypervolume improvement, Pareto-frontier quality)
Computational efficiency (e.g., analytical tractability, computation time)
novelty_claims: Introduction of Pareto-frontier entropy search (PFES), which directly considers the entropy of the Pareto-frontier, capturing the essential trade-off relations among objectives in multi-objective optimization
Derivation of an analytical formula for Pareto-frontier entropy without additional approximations or simplifications, unlike previous methods such as PESMO
Efficient computation of Pareto-frontier entropy using recursive decomposition techniques from Pareto hyper-volume computation, making the approach practical for higher-dimensional objectives

Simple and justified extension of PFES to the decoupled setting, allowing for separate observation of objectives without additional approximations, and capturing dependencies among objectives conditioned on the Pareto-frontier

Presents a simulation-based method for unbiased gradient estimation for the EI-UU acquisition function.
